ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific salted-egg-coated deep-fried squid entry exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers processed squid and cuttlefish products; no salted-egg-specific sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for salted egg squid dish found in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of salted egg squid is approximately 150 g, equivalent to roughly 6–8 pieces of battered squid rings or tentacles coated in salted egg sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Salted Egg Squid — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F03060021 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg squid. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being a cornstarch-based batter coating, analogous to battered/deep-fried starchy coatings with GI ~65 (medium) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for cornstarch and similar refined-starch coatings. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 65 × net carbohydrate per serving (17.72 g/100g × 150 g serving = 26.58 g net carbs) / 100 = 17.3 × 0.65 ≈ 11.3; however, accounting for the high fat content which substantially slows gastric emptying and blunts glycaemic response, an effective GL of approximately 7.7 per 150 g serving is used. Estimation documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 508 mg/100 g from salted duck egg yolk and added salt; saturated fat ~10.5 g/100 g contributes to arterial stiffness; potassium ~180 mg/100 g is insufficient to offset sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit salted egg squid to no more than 40–50 g per occasion, avoid additional dipping sauces or condiments, and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake carefully given the 508 mg/100 g sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total fat 29.4 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~10.5 g/100 g worsens insulin resistance; cornstarch batter coating provides refined carbohydrate with medium GI (~65); energy density 379 kcal/100 g is unfavourable for glycaemic and weight management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit salted egg squid to a very small portion (40–50 g), preferring steamed or grilled squid preparations without salted egg coating to reduce saturated fat and refined carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~10.5 g/100 g raises LDL cholesterol; estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g from deep-frying oil is atherogenic; sodium 508 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote vascular inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid salted egg squid due to its high saturated fat (~10.5 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), and sodium (508 mg/100 g) content, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles, blood pressure, and vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; saturated fat ~10.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g pro-inflammatory threshold; estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g is a hard pro-inflammatory disqualifier; sodium 508 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid salted egg squid, particularly during flares, and choose steamed or grilled squid without salted egg yolk coating to minimise AGE exposure, saturated fat, and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted egg squid (咸蛋鱿鱼; Sotong Telur Masin) is a popular Singaporean seafood dish consisting of fresh squid — typically rings and tentacles — that is lightly coated in cornstarch batter and deep-fried until crispy, then tossed in a rich, aromatic sauce made from mashed salted duck egg yolks, butter, curry leaves, and chilli. The dish delivers a distinctive flavour profile: intensely savoury and umami-rich from the salted egg yolk, with a creamy, slightly sandy coating texture and a subtle heat from the chilli. The squid itself remains tender inside while the batter provides a light crunch. Nutritionally, salted egg squid is energy-dense at 379 kcal per 100 g, with high total fat (29.4 g/100 g) dominated by estimated saturated fat (~10.5 g/100 g) from the egg yolk and butter, moderate protein (10.9 g/100 g) from the squid, and moderate carbohydrate (17.7 g/100 g) from the cornstarch coating. Sodium is elevated at 508 mg/100 g, driven by the salted duck egg yolk and seasoning. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat, sodium, and deep-frying-derived AGE content. It is widely served in Singaporean hawker centres, zi char restaurants, and as a popular sharing dish at family meals and social gatherings. The dish has gained significant popularity across Southeast Asia and is commonly found in Malaysian and Indonesian Chinese restaurants as well.

Can diabetics eat salted egg squid?

What is salted egg squid made of?
Is salted egg squid suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F03060021, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated at ~10.5 g/100 g based on typical salted duck egg yolk and deep-frying oil composition; trans fat estimated at ~0.1 g/100 g from deep-frying; potassium estimated at ~180 mg/100 g from squid baseline. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database cornstarch/battered-food references. Ontology codes are defensible approximations with gap notes provided.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g (10.5 g/100 g): estimated from typical salted duck egg yolk (~17 g sat fat/100 g yolk) and deep-frying oil contribution; not directly reported in HPB source. 2. trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g): estimated from deep-frying oil degradation; not directly reported in HPB source. 3. potassium_mg (180 mg/100 g): estimated from squid baseline data in USDA FoodData Central (squid, mixed species, raw: ~246 mg/100 g, reduced for processing losses); not directly reported in HPB source. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific preparation not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 5. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this dish and detailed compositional data for this specific preparation is not available in any approved source as of 2026-04-16. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~65, medium) based on cornstarch-battered deep-fried food analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for salted egg squid exists in any approved GI source. 7. Dietary tag 'wholegrain' from input cultural.dietary_tags has been removed as it is factually incorrect for this deep-fried cornstarch-battered dish. 8. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from input has been removed as salted egg squid is not a typical breakfast food in Singapore; it is primarily a lunch/dinner zi char or hawker dish. 9. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315763 is the closest defensible anchor for squid food product; no exact salted-egg-coated preparation class exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04-16. 10. FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers processed squid/cuttlefish; no salted-egg-specific sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
